citations.narrative:
At the time of inspection, the licensee did not have complete records demonstrating the type and number of animals and
birds on hand. There were 17 domestic rabbits and 22 domestic chickens present at the facility at the time of the
inspection. However, there were only 7 rabbits and 13 chickens identified by the licensee’s acquisition records.
An accurate animal inventory is required so that USDA inspectors can ensure all animals are accounted for and are
receiving adequate veterinary care. Accurate records are also important in tracing animals in a disease outbreak event.
The licensee must keep and maintain records or forms which fully and correctly disclose the animals acquired and
disposed of (including births/hatchings and deaths), in accordance with 2.75(b)(1)(i) to (vii).
To be corrected by close of business on 2/6/2026.

At the time of the inspection, the license was not able to provide a documented contingency plan for the facility. Without
adequate contingency planning, the animals at the facility may not be provided with appropriate protection and care in the
event of an emergency.
The licensee must develop, document, and follow an appropriate contingency plan [in accordance with 2.134(a)(1) to (4)]
to provide for the humane handling, treatment, transportation, housing, and care of their animals in the event of an
emergency or disaster (one which could reasonably be anticipated and expected to be detrimental to the good health and
well-being of the animals in their possession).
To be corrected by close of business on 2/6/2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the licensee.

narrative:
2.75(b)(1) 
   
Records: Dealers and exhibitors. 
At the time of inspection, the licensee did not have complete records demonstrating the type and number of animals and 
birds on hand. There were 17 domestic rabbits and 22 domestic chickens present at the facility at the time of the 
inspection. However, there were only 7 rabbits and 13 chickens identified by the licensee’s acquisition records. 
 
An accurate animal inventory is required so that USDA inspectors can ensure all animals are accounted for and are 
receiving adequate veterinary care. Accurate records are also important in tracing animals in a disease outbreak event. 
 
The licensee must keep and maintain records or forms which fully and correctly disclose the animals acquired and 
disposed of (including births/hatchings and deaths), in accordance with 2.75(b)(1)(i) to (vii).  
 
To be corrected by close of business on 2/6/2026. 
2.134(a) 
   
Contingency planning. 
At the time of the inspection, the license was not able to provide a documented contingency plan for the facility. Without 
adequate contingency planning, the animals at the facility may not be provided with appropriate protection and care in the 
event of an emergency. 
 
The licensee must develop, document, and follow an appropriate contingency plan [in accordance with 2.134(a)(1) to (4)] 
to provide for the humane handling, treatment, transportation, housing, and care of their animals in the event of an 
emergency or disaster (one which could reasonably be anticipated and expected to be detrimental to the good health and 
well-being of the animals in their possession). 
 
To be corrected by close of business on 2/6/2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the licensee.
